planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; obedient plant spreads aggressively by rhizomes in moist, fertile soil — site it where this can be accommodated or divide yearly to control.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; obedient plant is a native eastern North American perennial that fills a critical late-season gap in the shade garden by producing dense spikes of snapdragon-like flowers in bright rose-pink, lavender, or white from late summer through fall — among the best native plants for this late-season color window. The flowers can be repositioned on the stem and stay in the new position, which accounts for the common name 'obedient.' The plant spreads vigorously by rhizomes in fertile, moist soil and benefits from division every 2–3 years to maintain vigor and prevent the center from dying out. Excellent for attracting bumblebees and hummingbirds in fall. 'Vivid' (compact, bright pink) and 'Miss Manners' (white, more restrained spreader) are favored cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; Indian pink is slow to establish but extremely long-lived once settled and rarely needs division.

directions: Plant divisions or container specimens in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Indian pink (pinkroot) is one of the most spectacular native wildflowers of the eastern and southeastern United States, producing erect stems hung with long, tubular, brilliant scarlet-red flowers that open at the tip to reveal a bright yellow, five-pointed star interior — a bicolor display that is custom-built to attract ruby-throated hummingbirds and is among the most eye-catching effects possible in a shaded woodland garden. Blooms in late spring to early summer. The plant is slow from seed but extremely long-lived and reliable once established, forming clumps that gradually expand over years. All plant parts are toxic. Not widely available in commerce — source from native plant nurseries.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Transplant rooted stem cuttings or small clumps in moist, very acidic, humus-rich soil in full shade in spring; partridgeberry demands acid soil (pH 4.5–5.5) and is best established in prepared, pine-bark-enriched beds under large oaks, pines, or rhododendrons.

directions: Transplant rooted cuttings or small clumps in moist, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in full shade in spring; partridgeberry is a native evergreen groundcover of eastern North American forest floors that forms a tight, creeping mat of small, paired, round, glossy dark-green leaves marked with pale veins, producing pairs of tiny, fused, fragrant white tubular flowers in summer followed by a single, paired-scar, brilliant-red berry that persists through winter and is prized food for grouse, turkeys, foxes, and songbirds. The berries are edible to humans and mildly pleasant-tasting, though rarely produced in quantity. It is an exquisite but demanding terrarium and woodland garden plant requiring deep shade, consistently moist acid soil, and excellent drainage. One of the finest fine-textured native groundcovers for the darkest spots.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade in spring; fringed bleeding heart remains in bloom from spring through fall in cool climates and does not go summer-dormant like the common bleeding heart.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; fringed bleeding heart is a native Appalachian perennial that surpasses the common bleeding heart (Lamprocapnos spectabilis) in most garden settings by blooming continuously from spring through hard frost in cool climates — the deeply ferny, blue-gray, dissected foliage is attractive all season and never goes summer-dormant. The dangling, heart-shaped, rose-pink flowers appear in loose, nodding clusters on arching stems. It tolerates more heat and summer drought than the common bleeding heart. Self-sows gently to maintain a naturalized presence in woodland gardens without becoming invasive. Excellent in combination with hostas, ferns, and astilbes for a year-round shade border. Hybrids such as 'King of Hearts' (compact, deep rose) and 'Luxuriant' (strong bloomer) often replace the straight species in commerce.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in moist to average, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Eastern bluestar is adaptable to a wide range of conditions including clay soil, occasional wet feet, and light shade under deciduous trees.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to average, well-drained soil in partial shade to full sun; Eastern bluestar is a superb, long-lived native perennial that delivers three distinct seasons of ornamental value — clusters of small, pale powder-blue, star-shaped flowers in mid-spring, dense, shrubby, fine-textured mounds of glossy foliage that never need staking or deadheading all summer, and spectacular golden-yellow fall color that rivals even ornamental shrubs. It is unusually adaptable, performing well in clay, moist soil, partial shade, or full sun. The milky sap is mildly irritating; wear gloves when dividing. Shear plants back by one-third after bloom to maintain compact form. 'Storm Cloud' and 'Blue Ice' (dwarf) are cultivated selections. Deer-resistant, drought-tolerant once established, and virtually problem-free.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
